subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 4 periods = 160 minutes
Total: 4 periods · 2h 40min

periods.activity_title: King Bruce and the Spider: First Reading and Summary

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Pre-reading: who inspires you to do your best?
Fill-in summary of the poem using box words
Complete sentences: narrative style and central idea

phases.description: Pre-reading warm-up: students share who or what inspires them to do their best (page 16). Teacher takes three or four responses and links the idea of unlikely sources of inspiration to the poem's spider.

Teacher reads the poem aloud with expression, emphasising the mounting tension as the spider attempts climb after climb. Students follow in their textbooks, noting the ABAB rhyme scheme and the repeated phrases 'tried and tried', 'up, up', 'steadily, steadily'.

Students complete the fill-in summary (page 19) using the eight box words (tried, mounted, despair, attempts, great, conquered, low, strive), then complete the two sentence-completion items on narrative style and central idea (page 19). Teacher corrects on the board.

Teacher introduces the term 'extended metaphor' briefly — the spider's repeated climbs mirror Bruce's own repeated failures — and previews that Period 7 will examine the poem's literary devices in depth.

periods.teacher_notes: Read the poem at a measured pace; the cumulative tension of the spider's nine attempts is best felt when the teacher slows slightly at each fall and picks up pace on each new ascent. The fill-in summary anchors comprehension before literary analysis begins. Pre-reading tasks II (pictures of nature) and the later extract tasks are not anchored in this period; flag extract analysis as the focus for Period 7.

periods.activity_title: Repetition, Metaphor, and the Spider's Defiance of Despair

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Pick examples: spider's efforts and alliteration
Why does the poet repeat 'tried and tried'; 'steadily, steadily'; 'up, up'?
Identify metaphors and similes in nine sentences

homework.task_brief: Let us discuss (p.19): Fill in three MCQ items on rhyme scheme and poetic devices

phases.description: Students pick three examples from the poem for lines describing the spider's efforts and three examples of alliteration (page 19). Pairs compare choices before a brief class share.

Teacher leads discussion on why the poet repeats 'tried and tried', 'steadily, steadily', and 'up, up' (page 19). Students write an extended answer; teacher guides them to see that repetition enacts — not merely describes — the persistence it celebrates.

Teacher introduces metaphor vs simile using the poem's spider-as-Bruce comparison as the anchor, then students read nine sentences and label each M or S (page 20). Teacher corrects on the board and asks students to justify two choices.

Homework assigned: complete the three fill-in MCQ items on rhyme scheme, 'Bravo' as interjection, and 'twas/'tis for rhythm (page 19). Teacher previews that Period 8 will cover listening, speaking and the letter-writing task.

periods.teacher_notes: The repetition question is the conceptual centrepiece of this period — guide students beyond 'the poet repeats for emphasis' to the richer idea that the rhythm of repetition mirrors the spider's physical rhythm of climb and fall. Task VI.2 (transforming similes to metaphors) is omitted from class due to time; it can be assigned as extension homework for stronger students. Extract I and II close-reading questions are also left as self-study; flag them in teacher notes.

periods.activity_title: The Persistent Ant: Listening and Personal Narration

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Predict then verify: five MCQs on the ant story
Narrate a personal perseverance experience using prompts

phases.description: Students read the five MCQ questions about the ant story silently and make predictions before listening (page 24). Teacher asks two students to share their predictions and reasoning.

Teacher reads/plays the ant story (transcript p.40) at natural pace. Students listen and check their predicted answers, then verify as a class. Teacher briefly draws the parallel between the ant's determination and the spider's in the poem.

Speaking task: students narrate a personal experience of perseverance using the seven-step prompt framework — Introduction, Describe the challenge, Feeling disheartened, The source of motivation, Taking action, The result, Reflection (page 25). Three or four students present; peers listen actively.
Teacher gives brief oral feedback on two narrations — noting strong use of transition language and vivid detail — and previews that Period 9 will cover vocabulary, grammar, and writing for Section B.

periods.teacher_notes: The prediction step before listening is important: it activates prior knowledge and gives students a purpose for listening. For the speaking task, ensure every student who speaks uses at least three of the seven prompt steps; the 'Source of motivation' step is most directly linked to the poem's theme. Listening Task II (re-listen and verify) is folded into the class correction after the first play to save time.

periods.activity_title: Antonyms, Distance Words, and a Letter of Perseverance

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Write opposites of five poem words
Classify distance words as far or near
Write letter to cousin on overcoming difficulty, using transition words

phases.description: Students write the opposites of five words drawn from the poem — up, fast, glad, win, succeed (page 22). Teacher corrects quickly and then asks students to locate two of these words in the poem to see them in their emotional context.

Teacher explains the poem's distance language ('a long way up', 'inch by inch', 'half-yard higher') before students classify the box words and phrases as far or near (page 23). Teacher corrects on the board and briefly explains 'proximity', 'vicinity', 'yonder', and 'afar'.

Writing task: teacher displays the letter scaffold on the board (opening, three body paragraphs with transition words, closing) and students write a letter to their cousin on how to overcome a difficult situation, using 'To begin with', 'Next', 'After that', 'Then', 'Finally' as structural markers (page 26). Teacher circulates and gives formative feedback on format and transition word use.
Teacher asks one volunteer to read their opening paragraph aloud and gives one targeted compliment and one improvement suggestion before transitioning to Section C in Period 10.

periods.teacher_notes: Vocabulary Tasks I and III are kept short to protect the writing time — the letter is the period's main output and requires at least 28 minutes for a draft of reasonable quality. Task II (word meanings and own sentences for endeavour, toil, strive, braced) and Task IV (tongue twisters) are good self-study candidates; mention them briefly as extension. Remind students that the letter format — date, greeting, transition words, closing — is a functional writing skill they will use across subjects.

items.source_lo: Student demonstrates understanding of a narrative poem by identifying its central idea, literary devices (repetition, metaphor, simile), and the poet's purpose in crafting them.
items.item_stem:
Read the following stanza from 'Try Again' by Eliza Cook:

'Up, up it ran, not a second to stay,
To utter the least complaint;
Till it fell still lower, and there it lay,
A little dizzy and faint.'

(a) The poet uses the phrase 'Up, up' in this stanza. Identify the literary device used and explain the effect it creates in conveying the spider's determination.
(b) The spider is described as 'a little dizzy and faint' after falling. How does this description make the spider's eventual success more meaningful?
(c) King Bruce watches the spider and eventually cries 'Bravo, bravo! All honour to those who try.' What does Bruce's change from despair to celebration tell us about the poem's central message?

expected_elements: Identifies 'Up, up' as repetition; explains it mirrors the spider's physical rhythm of persistent effort.
Notes that the spider's vulnerability (dizzy, faint) makes its tenth success feel earned rather than easy.
Links Bruce's transformation from 'I'll give it all up' to 'why shouldn't I?' as the poem's perseverance message.
Connects the spider as an extended metaphor for Bruce's own repeated defeats and final resolve.

items.question_text:
Read the following stanza from 'Try Again' by Eliza Cook:

'Up, up it ran, not a second to stay,
To utter the least complaint;
Till it fell still lower, and there it lay,
A little dizzy and faint.'

(a) The poet uses the phrase 'Up, up' in this stanza. Identify the literary device used and explain the effect it creates in conveying the spider's determination.
(b) The spider is described as 'a little dizzy and faint' after falling. How does this description make the spider's eventual success more meaningful?
(c) King Bruce watches the spider and eventually cries 'Bravo, bravo! All honour to those who try.' What does Bruce's change from despair to celebration tell us about the poem's central message?

items.source_lo: Student demonstrates comprehension of a spoken narrative by predicting answers to inference-based questions before listening and verifying them after.
items.item_stem:
You will listen to a short story about a determined ant who tries to carry a piece of roti home. Before listening, write what you predict for each question. After listening, check and correct your predictions.

1. The ant in the story was known for its — (a) strength  (b) determination  (c) wisdom
   My prediction: _______   After listening: _______

2. The ant could not carry the roti home at first because — (a) it was too heavy  (b) other ants were pulling it  (c) it was stuck under a rock
   My prediction: _______   After listening: _______

3. The bird in the story was — (a) searching for food  (b) playing with the ant  (c) picking up leaves
   My prediction: _______   After listening: _______

4. After watching the bird, the ant felt — (a) proud  (b) relaxed  (c) inspired
   My prediction: _______   After listening: _______

5. The fellow ants helped by — (a) advising the ant  (b) standing still  (c) joining the ant's efforts
   My prediction: _______   After listening: _______

options.text: it was too heavy to carry
by joining the ant in her efforts

teacher_guide.note: [Verification failed] Correct answers for questions 1–5 should be verified against the listening transcript at p.40 before administering this item.

items.source_lo: Student delivers a structured personal oral narrative using guided prompts, organising ideas with appropriate sequencing and personal reflection.
items.item_stem:
King Bruce of Scotland was on the verge of giving up when a spider's repeated attempts to reach its cobweb home inspired him to try once more. Think of a time in your own life when you felt like giving up but chose to keep going — or a time when you saw someone else persist through difficulty.

Speak for two minutes on this experience. Organise your narration using these stages:
1. Introduction — describe the situation briefly.
2. The challenge — what made it difficult?
3. The turning point — what or who inspired you to keep going?
4. The outcome — what happened when you persisted?
5. Reflection — what did this experience teach you about failure and perseverance?

Use transition phrases such as 'To begin with', 'Then', 'At that point', and 'Looking back'.

expected_elements: Narration follows the five-stage structure in the correct sequence.
Identifies a clear turning point or source of motivation.
Uses at least two transition phrases to signal movement between stages.
Reflection stage offers a genuine insight about failure or perseverance.
Maintains appropriate pace and addresses the audience, not the notes.

items.question_text:
King Bruce of Scotland was on the verge of giving up when a spider's repeated attempts to reach its cobweb home inspired him to try once more. Think of a time in your own life when you felt like giving up but chose to keep going — or a time when you saw someone else persist through difficulty.

Speak for two minutes on this experience. Organise your narration using these stages:
1. Introduction — describe the situation briefly.
2. The challenge — what made it difficult?
3. The turning point — what or who inspired you to keep going?
4. The outcome — what happened when you persisted?
5. Reflection — what did this experience teach you about failure and perseverance?

Use transition phrases such as 'To begin with', 'Then', 'At that point', and 'Looking back'.

items.source_lo: Student writes a formal personal letter using functional format conventions and transition words to organise advice in a logical sequence.
items.item_stem:
King Bruce of Scotland nearly gave up on his great deed but was inspired by a spider's nine failed attempts before its final success. Imagine you have a younger cousin who is preparing for a difficult school competition and has written to you saying she wants to quit because she keeps failing her practice sessions.

Write a letter (120–150 words) to your cousin advising her not to give up. In your letter:
— Use the correct letter format: your address, date, greeting, body paragraphs, and closing.
— Refer to the spider's story from 'Try Again' as an example of perseverance.
— Use transition words (To begin with / Next / After that / Finally) to organise your advice.
— End with an encouraging closing thought.

Begin: [Your address] / [Date] / Dear [Name], / Thank you for your letter...

expected_elements: Correct letter format: address, date, greeting, at least two body paragraphs, and closing.
At least one reference to the spider's perseverance from the poem, used as evidence.
Three or more transition words used to sequence the advice logically.
Tone is warm, encouraging, and appropriate for writing to a younger cousin.
Closing thought is original and motivating, not a repetition of the opening.

items.question_text:
King Bruce of Scotland nearly gave up on his great deed but was inspired by a spider's nine failed attempts before its final success. Imagine you have a younger cousin who is preparing for a difficult school competition and has written to you saying she wants to quit because she keeps failing her practice sessions.

— Use the correct letter format: your address, date, greeting, body paragraphs, and closing.
— Refer to the spider's story from 'Try Again' as an example of perseverance.
— Use transition words (To begin with / Next / After that / Finally) to organise your advice.
— End with an encouraging closing thought.

Begin: [Your address] / [Date] / Dear [Name], / Thank you for your letter...

items.source_lo: Student identifies antonyms and classifies words by distance (near/far), demonstrating understanding of vocabulary related to spatial and directional contrast.
items.item_stem: Eliza Cook's poem 'Try Again' is built on contrast — the spider falls down but climbs up, it is near the ground and then far above it, the king sinks into despair and then rises with resolve. Write the antonym (opposite) for each word from the poem, as it is used in the poem, in the table below.
items.visual_stimulus:
Word from poem|Antonym
low (as in 'low despair')|
faint (as in 'dizzy and faint')|
conquered|
mounted (climbed upward)|
brave (as in 'nine brave attempts')|

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: low → high; faint → strong/sturdy; conquered → surrendered/failed; mounted → descended/fell; brave → cowardly/timid.

items.question_text: Eliza Cook's poem 'Try Again' is built on contrast — the spider falls down but climbs up, it is near the ground and then far above it, the king sinks into despair and then rises with resolve. Write the antonym (opposite) for each word from the poem, as it is used in the poem, in the table below.
